answer:
There is research, but not a perfect study of “OpenAI-interesting IC/research role vs Google-higher-pay/team-lead role.” The closest evidence comes from job-design, person–job fit, intrinsic motivation, pay/well-being, and regret/affective-forecasting literatures.

My read of the evidence: **interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work is a major predictor of job satisfaction and well-being, often stronger than incremental pay once you are already well paid.** But “leading a team” can itself be intrinsically rewarding if it gives you autonomy, impact, learning, and identity fit. So the key is not “interesting work vs management” but **which role will give you more day-to-day intrinsic motivation, autonomy, growth, social fit, and meaning.**

## 1. Work content and job design matter a lot

A large literature starting with Hackman & Oldham’s **Job Characteristics Model** finds that people are more motivated and satisfied when jobs have:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback.

These are basically components of “interesting work,” though also broader than that.

A major meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** integrated hundreds of work-design studies and found that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — are strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower stress/turnover intentions.

Relatedly, **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, a meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model, found consistent positive relationships between enriched job characteristics and job satisfaction/motivation.

So the research answer is: yes, the nature of the work itself is one of the major determinants of job satisfaction.

## 2. Person–job fit is one of the strongest predictors

“Interesting work” may be better framed as **person–job fit**: does the role fit your abilities, values, preferred activities, and motives?

A widely cited meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that person–job fit is strongly related to:

- job satisfaction,
- organizational commitment,
- lower intent to quit,
- performance.

The correlation with job satisfaction is large by organizational-psychology standards — around the .5 range in many analyses.

This is directly relevant. If OpenAI gives you high person–job fit because you love the research content, that is a serious factor. But if you have a strong latent identity around “I want to lead, mentor, set direction, build teams,” then the Google role may improve fit along a different dimension.

## 3. Intrinsic motivation predicts satisfaction and performance

Self-determination theory, especially **Deci & Ryan**, argues that people thrive when work satisfies three needs:

- autonomy,
- competence,
- relatedness.

Work that is interesting usually satisfies autonomy and competence. Leading a team may satisfy autonomy, relatedness, and impact — if the role is designed well.

A meta-analysis by **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014** found that intrinsic motivation is a meaningful predictor of performance, especially performance quality. Extrinsic incentives also matter, but intrinsic motivation is not just “nice to have”; it predicts real outcomes.

So if the Google role is “less interesting” in a deep sense — fewer competence-growth loops, less curiosity, less flow — that is a meaningful cost.

## 4. Pay matters, but the marginal effect is usually modest at high income levels

The evidence on salary is more nuanced.

A meta-analysis by **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010** found that the relationship between pay level and job satisfaction is positive but relatively small. A common takeaway from that literature is: pay matters, especially if you are underpaid or financially insecure, but beyond a comfortable level, incremental pay has weaker effects than work content, autonomy, social environment, and meaning.

For overall well-being, **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rises with income up to a point, while life evaluation continues to rise. Later work by **Killingsworth, 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** revised the “plateau” story: happiness often continues rising with income, but roughly logarithmically and with heterogeneity. The marginal value of extra income is real but diminishing, especially for already high earners.

In your specific case — OpenAI vs Google, both likely high-compensation environments — “a bit higher salary” is probably less predictive of future happiness than daily work content, autonomy, team quality, and career identity fit.

## 5. Meaningful work and “calling” are strongly associated with well-being

Research on meaningful work finds robust associations with job satisfaction, engagement, and life satisfaction. For example, work by **Wrzesniewski et al.** on jobs/careers/callings and later meta-analytic work on meaningful work show that people who see their work as meaningful tend to report higher satisfaction and engagement.

But there is an important caveat: meaningful work can also make people tolerate worse conditions, overwork, or exploitation. So “interesting/meaningful” is valuable, but not if it comes with unsustainable demands or poor management.

In your context, if the OpenAI work feels unusually meaningful and rare, that weighs heavily. If it is merely “more technically fun” but the Google role gives you meaning through leadership and impact, the comparison is less obvious.

## 6. Promotions and leadership are mixed: more status/autonomy, but also more stress/admin

The literature on promotion and supervisory responsibility is not as clean. Promotions often increase job satisfaction, partly through status, pay, autonomy, and perceived progress. But management roles can also increase stress, interruptions, emotional labor, coordination costs, and distance from the technical work that originally motivated you.

So “leading a small team” could be a large positive if you genuinely enjoy:

- mentoring,
- prioritizing,
- giving feedback,
- resolving ambiguity,
- representing the team,
- hiring,
- aligning stakeholders,
- taking responsibility for others’ output.

But if what you mainly want is research depth, then management can reduce the very job characteristics that predict your satisfaction: deep work, technical novelty, direct problem-solving, and flow.

The research would say: evaluate the actual day-to-day role, not the title.

## 7. Regret research: people often regret missed “ideal self” opportunities

There is some relevant regret literature, though less job-specific.

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that people tend to regret actions more in the short term, but inactions more in the long term. **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that education and career are among the most common life-regret domains. **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018** found that people’s most enduring regrets often involve failures to live up to their “ideal self” — hopes, aspirations, and unrealized possibilities — more than failures of duty.

This cuts both ways for you.

You might regret leaving unusually interesting frontier research. But you might also regret not trying leadership if “I’ve always wanted to lead a team” is a deep ideal-self goal.

The regret literature does not say “choose the more interesting job.” It says: people often regret not pursuing personally meaningful possibilities. The hard question is which possible self matters more to you: frontier researcher/engineer, or technical leader/manager.

## 8. Affective forecasting: people overweight salient factors like salary/title

Research by **Wilson & Gilbert** on affective forecasting, and by **Kahneman** and colleagues on the focusing illusion, suggests people often overestimate how much a salient change will affect happiness. Salary, company prestige, and title are highly salient. Daily experience — meetings, autonomy, team quality, manager quality, commute, bureaucracy, flow — often matters more than people predict.

So a research-based decision rule would be: do not compare the jobs by offer-letter features. Compare them by predicted Tuesday afternoon.

Ask:

- What will I be doing 30 hours per week?
- How often will I experience curiosity, flow, or learning?
- How much autonomy will I have?
- Will I admire my manager?
- Will I like the people I work with?
- How much bureaucracy/stakeholder management is involved?
- How much of the leadership role is real leadership versus coordination/admin?
- Can I return to frontier research if I dislike management?
- Can I get leadership opportunities at OpenAI instead?

## Bottom line from the research

The empirical literature broadly supports these claims:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomy-rich work is a major predictor of job satisfaction.**
2. **Person–job fit is one of the strongest known predictors of satisfaction and retention.**
3. **Incremental pay has a weaker effect once financial comfort is already high.**
4. **Leadership can be deeply satisfying if it fits your motives, but title/status alone is not enough.**
5. **People often regret not pursuing personally meaningful aspirations, so your long-standing desire to lead should not be dismissed.**
6. **The best predictor is likely the expected day-to-day experience, not salary or prestige.**

If you believe the Google role is genuinely much less interesting day-to-day, and the salary difference is only modest, the research would generally caution against moving for pay/title alone. But if leading a small team is itself a major intrinsic goal and the role gives you autonomy, growth, and impact, then the move could be well aligned with the same psychological factors that make interesting work valuable.

answer:
Short version: **yes, there is fairly consistent research that “interesting / meaningful / autonomous work” is one of the bigger predictors of job satisfaction and is moderately related to overall happiness.** Salary matters too, but a *modest* salary increase at an already high income level usually predicts a much smaller well-being change than differences in day-to-day work quality.  

The caveat: “leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic perk. It can satisfy autonomy, competence, status, growth, and identity needs—so the real comparison is not “interesting work vs money,” but **interesting individual-contributor research vs a possibly less interesting domain but more leadership/autonomy/growth**.

## 1. Work content, autonomy, and fit are strong predictors of job satisfaction

A lot of organizational psychology converges on the idea that people are happier in jobs with:

- autonomy/control,
- skill variety,
- learning and challenge,
- task significance/meaning,
- feedback,
- social support,
- fit between their interests/values and the role.

Classic framework: **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model**. Later meta-analyses, especially **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, find that job characteristics like autonomy, skill variety, task significance, feedback, and social support have substantial relationships with job satisfaction, motivation, and engagement. These are not tiny effects; many are in the moderate-to-large range for workplace psychology.

A particularly relevant line is **person–job fit**. In the meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, person–job fit correlated strongly with job satisfaction—roughly around **r ≈ .5** in corrected estimates—and negatively with intention to quit. That is much larger than the typical correlation between pay level and job satisfaction.

So, if “interesting work” means “the work fits my abilities/interests, I learn, I feel challenged, I have autonomy, and I care about the outcome,” then research would say: **yes, that matters a lot.**

## 2. Meaningful work is also strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical, but they often overlap in research jobs. Meta-analytic work on meaningful work—e.g. **Allan et al. 2019**—finds strong associations between meaningful work and:

- job satisfaction,
- engagement,
- organizational commitment,
- lower withdrawal intentions,
- general well-being.

Correlations between meaningful work and job satisfaction are often reported in the **r ≈ .5–.6** range, depending on measurement. Again: large by social-science standards.

For research/engineering specifically, intellectual challenge and autonomy seem especially important. One famous economics paper, **Stern 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”**, found that scientists were willing to accept lower wages for jobs that allowed them to do more “science-like” work—publication, intellectual freedom, participation in the scientific community. That is not directly a happiness study, but it is revealed-preference evidence that scientific workers value interesting/autonomous work enough to trade off money for it.

## 3. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller—especially at high income

There is also research on pay and job satisfaction. A useful meta-analysis is **Judge et al. 2010**, which found that absolute pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction—around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but even there the effects are usually smaller than those for job content, autonomy, fit, and meaning.

On general life satisfaction/happiness, income matters, but with diminishing returns or at least a roughly logarithmic relationship. The literature has some debate:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continued to rise with income, with no sharp plateau.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people happiness keeps rising with income, but the effect is modest and heterogeneous.

The practical implication for someone already in a high-paying research-engineering role: **a “bit higher” salary is unlikely to dominate your day-to-day well-being unless it changes something concrete—financial security, family plans, location, visa risk, ability to retire earlier, etc.**

Pay also matters psychologically through fairness/status. If you feel underpaid relative to peers, that can affect satisfaction more than the absolute dollar amount. But as a direct source of daily happiness, marginal pay increases are usually weaker than work quality.

## 4. But leading a team is a real factor, not just résumé/status

The Google offer includes something you “always wanted to do”: leading a small team. Research would not treat that as merely extrinsic. It could increase:

- autonomy,
- influence,
- responsibility,
- identity fit,
- sense of growth,
- relatedness/mentoring,
- task significance.

These are all well-supported predictors of work motivation and satisfaction. **Self-Determination Theory**, especially work by **Deci & Ryan** and meta-analytic work such as **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, argues that satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs is central to motivation and well-being at work.

So if becoming a team lead is deeply aligned with your desired identity and growth trajectory, the research says that can be a very meaningful source of satisfaction. It is not merely “more money and title.”

However, management also changes the activity mix. More meetings, coordination, performance management, hiring, prioritization, conflict resolution, politics, and less deep technical work. The key question is whether you are attracted to the **actual daily tasks of leading**, not just the idea of leadership.

## 5. Regret research: career regrets are common, but the evidence cuts both ways

I do not know of clean longitudinal studies that specifically compare people who chose “more interesting work” versus “higher salary / management” and then measure regret years later.

But adjacent regret research is relevant:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that career and education are among the most common domains of major life regret.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** found that in the long run people often regret inactions more than actions—missed opportunities loom large.
- **Davidai & Gilovich 2018** suggest that regrets related to the “ideal self”—the person you hoped to become—can be especially enduring.

This cuts both ways. If “I want to become a leader” is part of your ideal self, passing up a credible leadership opportunity may later feel like a missed path. But if “I want to work on the most interesting/frontier research problems” is core to your ideal self, leaving that for a less interesting role could also produce regret.

The research does *not* imply “always choose passion” or “always choose advancement.” It implies that regret is often tied to **foreclosed identity-relevant opportunities**.

## 6. Job changes often have a honeymoon effect

There is research on job changes showing a **honeymoon–hangover effect**—e.g. **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005**. People often become more satisfied immediately after changing jobs, but some of that boost fades as novelty wears off and the real work characteristics become apparent.

This is important because the Google offer may have short-term positives: external validation, higher comp, team lead title, novelty. Research suggests you should discount the novelty and ask: **what will the average Tuesday feel like after 12–18 months?**

## Evidence-weighted interpretation for your case

Based on the research, I would summarize the priors like this:

1. **If the salary increase is modest and you are already financially comfortable, it should probably get relatively low weight** as a happiness predictor.

2. **The interestingness/meaning/autonomy of the work should get high weight**, because those are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and are moderately linked to life satisfaction.

3. **The leadership opportunity should also get high weight if it genuinely satisfies a core goal**, because autonomy, competence growth, influence, and identity fit are major drivers of satisfaction.

4. The main danger in the Google option is not “less interesting” in the abstract; it is whether your daily work becomes dominated by things you find draining or low-meaning.

5. The main danger in staying is not “lower salary”; it is whether you later feel you avoided a scarce leadership transition you deeply wanted.

A useful research-informed question would be:

> Which option gives me more of the work characteristics that robustly predict satisfaction: autonomy, competence growth, meaning, interest fit, social fit, and sustainable demands?

Not:

> Which has the better title/salary/objective prestige?

Nor even:

> Which seems more exciting right now?

If possible, I would try to estimate the real activity allocation in each role: percentage of time on deep technical work, research direction-setting, mentoring, meetings, execution, politics, recruiting, product constraints, etc. The evidence suggests that this “average week” forecast is more predictive than the headline offer.

answer:
Yes — there is a fairly large research literature, though it usually studies “interesting work” under labels like **intrinsic motivation**, **meaningful work**, **job characteristics**, **person–job fit**, **autonomy**, and **calling**.

Short version: **for already well-paid professionals, a modest salary increase is usually a weaker predictor of happiness than work that is intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well-matched to the person.** But “interesting technical work” is not the only intrinsic factor: leading a team can itself satisfy autonomy, competence, growth, and identity needs.

## 1. Pay matters, but less than people expect at high income

A useful meta-analysis is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, *Journal of Vocational Behavior*: meta-analysis of pay and job satisfaction.  
  They found that the relationship between **pay level and overall job satisfaction is positive but small**, roughly around **r ≈ .15**. Pay is more strongly related to satisfaction with pay specifically, but only weakly related to overall job satisfaction.

This does not mean salary is irrelevant. It matters more when it affects security, fairness, status, family constraints, or freedom. But if you are already highly paid and the Google offer is only “a bit higher,” the expected happiness effect from money alone is likely modest.

Related subjective-well-being work:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**, PNAS: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, then flattened in their data.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**, PNAS: using experience sampling, found well-being continued to rise with income, but roughly logarithmically.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**, PNAS adversarial collaboration: income keeps helping most people, but the marginal effect is smaller, and for the least happy subgroup there may be a plateau.

The practical interpretation: at high compensation levels, a “bit higher salary” is unlikely to compensate for a large loss in daily engagement unless the money has special meaning for your life goals.

## 2. Intrinsic work features are strong predictors of job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980**: jobs are more motivating when they have skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback.

Meta-analytic support:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, *Personnel Psychology*: supported links between job characteristics and job satisfaction/motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*: meta-analysis showing that motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity — are robustly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower stress. The associations with satisfaction are often moderate to large compared with pay.

For your case, “interesting work” likely includes several of these: complexity, novelty, autonomy, feedback from reality, task significance, and opportunity to use rare skills. Losing those can matter a lot.

## 3. Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central

Another big literature is **Self-Determination Theory**:

- **Deci & Ryan, 1985; Ryan & Deci, 2000**
- Work-focused meta-analyses/reviews such as **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**

The core finding is that people tend to do better when work satisfies three needs:

1. **Autonomy** — I have real agency.
2. **Competence** — I am growing and using my abilities.
3. **Relatedness** — I feel connected to people I respect.

Interesting research work often satisfies autonomy and competence. But leading a small team might also satisfy them, especially if you have real decision authority and mentorship responsibilities. So the relevant comparison may not be “interesting work vs management”; it may be:

> Which role better satisfies autonomy, competence growth, relatedness, and meaning over the next few years?

## 4. Person–job fit is a large predictor

A very relevant result:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, *Personnel Psychology*: meta-analysis on person–job and person–organization fit.  
  Person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.

In plain language: people are happier when the job matches their abilities, values, and preferred activities.

This matters because you have two possible “fits”:

- Current role: strong fit with intellectual curiosity / frontier research.
- Google role: possibly stronger fit with leadership aspiration / team-building identity.

The research would not say “always choose the more intellectually interesting role.” It would say: choose the role with better **whole-person fit**.

## 5. Meaningful work is strongly related to satisfaction and well-being

There is a separate literature on meaningful work:

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work outcomes.
- **Steger, Dik & Duffy, 2012**, Work and Meaning Inventory and related research.

Meaningful work is consistently associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions. The effects are usually stronger than pay effects.

For someone in frontier AI research, “interesting” may also mean “socially consequential,” “historically important,” or “personally meaningful.” If the Google role feels much less meaningful, that is not a trivial cost.

But again, leadership can create meaning too: developing people, building a team, owning outcomes, shaping culture. You would want to know whether the Google role is merely “less interesting technically” or less meaningful overall.

## 6. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Job satisfaction is not just a workplace variable.

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**, meta-analysis: job satisfaction and life satisfaction are substantially related.
- Later work, e.g. **Bowling et al., 2010**, supports a meaningful relationship between job satisfaction and subjective well-being.

There is bidirectionality: happier people may be more satisfied at work, but work also affects overall life. Since most professionals spend a large fraction of waking hours working, daily work quality matters.

## 7. Regret research: people often regret inaction around important identities

Direct research on “do people regret choosing interesting work over money?” is thinner. But regret research gives useful clues.

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: short-term regrets are often about actions taken; long-term regrets are more often about actions not taken.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: people’s biggest regrets often involve domains with high perceived opportunity, including education, career, romance.
- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**: people especially regret failures to live up to their “ideal self” more than failures of duty/obligation.

For your decision, this cuts both ways.

You might later regret:

- “I had the chance to lead a team, something I always wanted, and I didn’t try.”
- Or: “I left unusually interesting frontier research for a more conventional role and lost the thing that energized me.”

The research suggests that regret is most likely when you forgo something tied to a central identity or ideal self. So the question is not just “which job is better?” but:

> Which unrealized path would feel more like betraying an important version of myself?

## 8. Management can increase autonomy and growth, but also stress

The leadership opportunity should not be treated as merely an extrinsic perk. In job-design terms, management can increase:

- autonomy,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- social impact,
- identity,
- future optionality.

But it can also increase:

- meetings,
- politics,
- role conflict,
- responsibility without authority,
- loss of deep technical work,
- stress.

This maps onto the **Job Demands–Resources model**:

- **Bakker & Demerouti, 2007** and later work: engagement is high when job resources — autonomy, support, feedback, growth — are high; burnout risk rises when demands are high and resources are insufficient.

So the key empirical question is not “is management good?” but:

> Is this specific management role high-resource or high-demand/low-resource?

A small team with real authority, good manager, strong mission, and room to shape direction could be fulfilling. A small team with weak mandate, boring roadmap, and political constraints may be a trap.

## 9. Research-backed heuristic for your case

Given the literature, I would summarize the evidence as:

1. **Do not overweight a modest salary increase** if you are already financially comfortable. Pay has a relatively small relationship with overall job satisfaction compared with intrinsic work quality and fit.
2. **Do heavily weight daily work content**, especially autonomy, challenge, meaning, and learning.
3. **Do heavily weight the leadership aspiration** if it is a deep, recurring goal rather than a vague status preference.
4. **Try to separate “less interesting domain” from “less interesting role.”** A technically less exciting area may still become interesting if you are building people, strategy, and systems.
5. **Consider reversibility.** If trying management for 18–24 months leaves you able to return to frontier research, regret risk is lower. If leaving makes it hard to re-enter the most interesting work, the cost is higher.
6. **Ask whether you can get the missing ingredient where you are.** If your current role is highly interesting and the missing piece is leadership, an internal path to leading a small team may dominate both options.

## A practical research-aligned checklist

For each role, rate 1–10:

| Dimension | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Intrinsic interest | Strong predictor of satisfaction/engagement |
| Autonomy | Central in job-design and SDT research |
| Competence growth | Important for motivation and long-term fit |
| Meaning/social significance | Strongly linked to satisfaction |
| Relatedness/team quality | Important for well-being |
| Manager quality | Major determinant of day-to-day experience |
| Workload/stress | Can dominate other factors |
| Leadership development | Important if central to your identity |
| Reversibility/option value | Reduces future regret |
| Compensation | Matters, but likely lower marginal impact here |

If Google only wins on pay and formal title, research would make me cautious.  
If Google wins on leadership, autonomy, growth, people you like, and a tolerable mission — while pay is also better — then the research case becomes much stronger.  
If OpenAI wins on interest/meaning and you can plausibly get leadership there soon, that may be the best of both worlds.

answer:
Yes. There is not much clean research on exactly “stay in the more interesting AI research job vs. take a slightly higher-paying less-interesting team-lead role,” but several adjacent literatures are quite relevant.

## Short answer

Research generally finds that **intrinsic job quality** — interesting work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, fit with one’s interests, good colleagues, and manageable stress — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and is meaningfully related to overall life satisfaction. **Pay matters**, but actual pay level usually has a weaker relationship with job satisfaction than people expect, especially once income is already high and basic/fairness needs are met.

However, “leading a small team” is not merely an extrinsic perk. It can satisfy autonomy, competence, growth, status, relatedness, and identity needs. So the key empirical question is not “interesting work vs. salary,” but:

> Would the Google role give you more of the psychological ingredients of good work, or mainly more money/title while reducing the day-to-day work you find meaningful?

## Findings worth knowing

### 1. Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

The classic literature is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham, later meta-analyzed by Fried & Ferris and by Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson.

Relevant job features include:

- autonomy  
- skill variety  
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- task significance — believing the work matters  
- feedback from the work itself  
- job complexity / challenge

Meta-analyses generally find medium-to-large associations between these features and job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal intentions. In practice, “interesting work” usually maps onto several of these at once: challenge, learning, autonomy, and perceived significance.

A useful reference:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, meta-analysis of work design research. Motivational job characteristics were among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and internal work motivation.

So yes: people in more interesting, autonomous, skill-using jobs tend to be more satisfied at work.

### 2. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Job satisfaction is not everything, but it is not isolated from overall happiness either.

Meta-analyses find correlations around the moderate range between **job satisfaction and life satisfaction / subjective well-being**. The relationship is bidirectional: happier people tend to be more satisfied at work, but better work also appears to improve well-being.

Relevant papers:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989** — meta-analysis on job and life satisfaction.  
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010** — meta-analysis showing job satisfaction is substantially related to subjective well-being.

For someone who spends a large fraction of waking hours thinking about work, the content of the work is likely to matter a lot.

### 3. Pay matters, but actual pay level is a weaker predictor than intrinsic job quality

A well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge et al., 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.”

They found that **actual pay level** has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, and fairness/comparison effects matter, but beyond some point, extra salary is not usually the main determinant of daily happiness.

On income and happiness more broadly:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.  
- **Killingsworth, 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** found that experienced well-being often continues rising with income, but roughly logarithmically: a 20% raise is much smaller psychologically than going from financial stress to comfort.

For someone already at OpenAI/Google compensation levels, the marginal happiness gain from “a bit higher salary” is probably much smaller than the gain/loss from changing the work itself, team quality, autonomy, stress, and sense of meaning.

### 4. Fit with interests and values is important

Person–job fit and person–organization fit are also robust predictors of satisfaction and retention.

Relevant paper:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis of person–environment fit.

They found strong relationships between person–job fit and job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and turnover intentions.

So if your current role is unusually well aligned with your intellectual interests, that is a serious factor. Conversely, if “becoming a team lead” is a deep personal value or identity goal, that also counts as fit. The research would not treat that as superficial.

### 5. Meaningful work is a major predictor of satisfaction and engagement

Another adjacent literature is **meaningful work**.

Relevant paper:

- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work.

Meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction, lower depression, and lower turnover intentions. This is especially relevant for research/engineering roles where the perceived importance of the work can be a big part of motivation.

If your current work feels like “I am doing some of the most important/interesting work available to me,” that is not a trivial perk. It is one of the central predictors of work well-being.

### 6. Intrinsic motivation matters especially for complex creative work

For routine work, extrinsic incentives can be very effective. For complex, creative, research-like work, intrinsic motivation tends to be especially important.

Relevant paper:

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014**, meta-analysis on intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives.

They found that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially for quality-oriented tasks. Extrinsic incentives can help, but they do not substitute perfectly for intrinsic motivation in complex work.

That matters here because research engineering is closer to creative problem-solving than routine execution. If the Google role is much less intellectually compelling, that could affect not only happiness but also performance and long-term career capital.

## What about regret?

The direct evidence is weaker. I do not know of a strong longitudinal study showing “people who chose interesting work over higher salary later regretted it less.” But regret research gives some useful clues.

### Career regrets are common

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that education, career, and romance are among the most common domains of life regret.

Career decisions are fertile ground for regret because they involve many counterfactual paths.

### Regrets of inaction often last longer than regrets of action

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that short-term regrets often involve actions taken, but long-term regrets more often involve inactions — things people did not try.

This cuts both ways in your case. You might later regret not trying leadership if it has been a long-standing aspiration. But you might also regret leaving unusually interesting frontier work if that is central to your identity.

### People especially regret failures to live up to their “ideal self”

- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018** found that enduring regrets often involve discrepancies from one’s ideal self — the person one wished one had become — more than from one’s “ought” self.

So the useful question is:

> Which path is more connected to your ideal self: being at the frontier doing deeply interesting research engineering, or becoming the kind of person who leads and grows a team?

That is likely more predictive of future regret than the salary difference.

## How I would translate the research into your decision

The evidence suggests:

1. **Do not overweight the salary bump** if it is only “a bit higher” and you are already financially comfortable.  
2. **Take the loss of interesting work seriously**. That is one of the best-supported predictors of job satisfaction.  
3. **Do take the team-lead opportunity seriously** if leadership is a genuine intrinsic goal, not just status. Leadership can provide autonomy, growth, identity, and impact.  
4. **Focus on the lived weekly experience**, not the abstract job title.

A research-grounded comparison would ask:

| Dimension | OpenAI role | Google role |
|---|---:|---:|
| Intellectual interest | ? | ? |
| Autonomy/control | ? | ? |
| Meaning/impact | ? | ? |
| Learning/growth | ? | ? |
| Leadership/mentorship | ? | ? |
| Quality of colleagues/manager | ? | ? |
| Stress/hours/politics | ? | ? |
| Compensation/fairness | ? | ? |
| Future optionality | ? | ? |

The literature would predict higher happiness from the role with better combined scores on **autonomy, meaning, skill use, social environment, and fit**, not necessarily the role with the highest salary.

## Practical implication

If the Google role is mainly:

> less interesting work + slightly more money + nominal leadership,

then the research would make me cautious.

If it is:

> somewhat less interesting technical domain + real autonomy + real mentorship/leadership + strong team + future path to interesting work,

then it may be genuinely competitive, because leadership itself can be an intrinsically meaningful form of work.

The key is to determine whether “leading a small team” is a deep source of motivation for you or mostly an attractive label attached to less compelling day-to-day work. The research says the former matters a lot; the latter usually fades.

answer:
Short version: **yes, the research literature generally finds that “interesting work” — usually operationalized as autonomy, challenge, skill use, variety, meaning, and fit with one’s interests — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being.** Pay matters, but once income is already high enough for security/comfort, marginal pay increases tend to be weaker predictors than intrinsic job qualities. Direct research on *regretting* choosing interesting work over money is thinner, but regret studies suggest people often regret missed growth/career opportunities more than modest financial tradeoffs.

A few relevant bodies of evidence:

---

## 1. Intrinsic job quality is a major predictor of job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It argues that jobs are motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**

They find that jobs with more autonomy, complexity, feedback, task significance, and variety are substantially associated with higher job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and lower turnover intentions. The effects are often moderate-to-large by organizational psychology standards — commonly larger than the raw association between salary and job satisfaction.

So “interesting work” is not just a luxury variable; in the literature it is close to the core of what makes a job satisfying.

---

## 2. Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are central to well-being at work

**Self-Determination Theory**, associated with Deci & Ryan, argues that people do better when work satisfies three needs:

- **autonomy** — I have agency/control  
- **competence** — I am growing and using my abilities  
- **relatedness** — I am connected to others  

A meta-analysis by **Van den Broeck et al., 2016** found that satisfaction of these needs at work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, vitality, and lower burnout.

For your case, this is important because “leading a small team” may satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness even if the underlying technical work is less interesting. So the choice is not simply:

> interesting work vs salary

It may be more like:

> deep technical/research interest vs leadership/autonomy/growth/status/pay

The research would treat both “interesting technical work” and “meaningful leadership responsibility” as potentially intrinsic motivators.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly linked to well-being

A meta-analysis by **Allan et al., 2019** on meaningful work found that people who see their work as meaningful report higher:

- job satisfaction  
- work engagement  
- life satisfaction  
- organizational commitment  

and lower:

- depression  
- withdrawal intentions  
- burnout-related outcomes  

This matters because “interesting” and “meaningful” often overlap but are not identical. A job can be intellectually fascinating but politically frustrating or low-impact; another can be less technically novel but meaningful because you mentor people, shape strategy, or create leverage.

---

## 4. Person–job fit and interest fit matter

There is also a large literature on **person–environment fit** and **vocational interest congruence**.

Relevant meta-analyses include:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** — person–job and person–organization fit predict job satisfaction, commitment, and lower intent to quit.
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012** — interest congruence predicts satisfaction, performance, and persistence, though effect sizes are moderate.

The practical interpretation is: people tend to be happier and stay longer when their work matches their interests, values, and preferred way of using their abilities.

So if the Google role is genuinely misaligned with what you find energizing, that is a serious negative predictor. But if “leading a team” is itself a strong interest/value for you, that also counts as fit.

---

## 5. Pay matters, but usually less than people expect once basic needs are met

There are two separate questions:

### Pay and job satisfaction

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al., 2010** found that pay level is only weakly to modestly related to job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .15** in many estimates. Pay satisfaction matters more than absolute pay, especially if people feel unfairly compensated, but raw salary is not usually among the strongest predictors of overall job satisfaction.

### Income and life satisfaction

The broader well-being literature finds that income is positively associated with life satisfaction, but with diminishing marginal returns.

Relevant studies:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** — emotional well-being rose with income up to a point in their U.S. data, while evaluative life satisfaction kept rising more steadily.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** — using experience sampling, found well-being continued to rise with income, with no sharp plateau.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** — reconciled some of the disagreement: for many people, well-being continues rising with income, but the slope is modest and varies by baseline happiness.

The important practical point is: at already-high tech/research salaries, a “bit higher salary” is likely to have less day-to-day well-being impact than a large change in work content, autonomy, manager quality, team culture, workload, or mission fit — unless the pay difference meaningfully changes your financial security, freedom, family situation, or sense of fairness.

---

## 6. Promotions and leadership are mixed: more status/autonomy, but also more stress

Research on promotion and managerial responsibility is more mixed.

Some studies find promotions increase job satisfaction because they bring:

- status  
- autonomy  
- income  
- influence  
- perceived progress  

But they can also increase:

- hours  
- stress  
- role conflict  
- administrative burden  
- responsibility for others’ problems  

For example, **Boyce & Oswald, 2012** found that promotion did not necessarily improve health and may worsen mental strain. Other panel studies, such as **Johnston & Lee, 2013**, suggest promotions can improve job satisfaction but may come with extra workload/stress.

So “leading a team” is not automatically good or bad. The key research-relevant questions are:

- Will you have real autonomy or just people-management overhead?  
- Will you still do intellectually engaging work?  
- Is the team high-quality?  
- Is your manager good?  
- Are you being set up to succeed?  
- Does leadership align with your identity and long-term goals?

---

## 7. Regret evidence: direct evidence is thin, but missed opportunities matter

There is not much clean research of the form:

> People who chose interesting work over higher pay later regretted/did not regret it.

But general regret research is relevant.

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: short-term regrets are often about actions taken; long-term regrets are more often about inactions — things not tried.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: common life regrets cluster around education, career, romance, and missed opportunities.

This does not imply “take the Google offer.” But it suggests that if leading a team is something you have always wanted, passing on a rare opportunity could become a salient regret — especially if you cannot easily get similar leadership opportunities later.

On the other hand, the regret literature does **not** strongly suggest people regret failing to maximize salary, especially when the salary difference is modest.

---

## Research-based synthesis for your specific tradeoff

Based on the literature, I would frame it this way:

1. **If the salary difference is only modest**, research suggests it should probably not dominate the decision, especially at high existing compensation levels.

2. **If the Google role is substantially less interesting day-to-day**, that is a real negative predictor of job satisfaction, not a superficial concern.

3. **But the leadership component may be intrinsically valuable**, not merely extrinsic. If leading a small team gives you autonomy, growth, influence, mentoring, and meaning, it may compensate for reduced technical novelty.

4. **Manager/team quality and autonomy may matter as much as abstract company prestige.** A fascinating job with poor autonomy or a bad manager can become miserable; a less glamorous job with high trust and agency can be satisfying.

5. **Regret risk may be highest around the path you feel you “never tried.”** If you have long wanted to lead, the research on regret/inaction suggests taking that seriously.

A useful evidence-based question is not “Which job is more prestigious/higher-paying?” but:

> In which role will my average Tuesday contain more autonomy, mastery, meaningful progress, good social interaction, and work I want to get better at?

Those repeated daily experiences are exactly the things the research says tend to drive job satisfaction.

answer:
Short version from the literature: **yes, “interesting work” — more precisely, work with autonomy, skill use, challenge, meaning, feedback, and person–job fit — is one of the stronger predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.** Pay matters, especially for security/fairness, but **at already-high income levels a moderate salary increase usually has smaller marginal effects on well-being than a large change in daily work quality.**  

The wrinkle in your case is that “leading a small team” may itself be intrinsically motivating if it gives you autonomy, impact, mentoring, and growth. So the relevant comparison may not be “interesting work vs. money,” but “technical/research interestingness vs. leadership/impact/autonomy interestingness.”

## 1. Interesting/intrinsic work is strongly associated with job satisfaction

A large organizational-psychology literature supports this.

### Job Characteristics Model  
Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model argues that jobs are more motivating/satisfying when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris 1987** and **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** find that these job characteristics are reliably related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. Autonomy and social support tend to be especially strong predictors.

In plain terms: people tend to be happier in jobs where they have agency, use valued skills, feel the work matters, and get feedback that they are making progress.

## 2. Person–job fit and interest fit matter

Research on **person–job fit** and vocational interest congruence also finds meaningful links to satisfaction.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** meta-analysis: person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.  
- **Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow 2012**: interest congruence predicts satisfaction, performance, and persistence, though effects are usually moderate rather than enormous.

So if your current role is unusually aligned with your intellectual interests and identity as a researcher/engineer, that is not a trivial variable. It is one of the things the literature would predict to matter.

## 3. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually smaller than people expect

The evidence on pay is nuanced.

### Pay and job satisfaction  
A useful meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They find that absolute pay level has only a relatively weak relationship with overall job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, especially perceived fairness.

Roughly: being underpaid, financially stressed, or unfairly compensated can hurt a lot. But once pay is already high and perceived as fair, another increment often has a limited effect on day-to-day happiness.

### Income and subjective well-being  
On general life satisfaction:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to around $75k/year in 2010 USD, while life evaluation kept rising.  
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the plateau finding, using experience sampling, and found experienced well-being continues to rise with income.  
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled the findings: for many people well-being keeps rising with income, but the relationship is roughly logarithmic and may flatten for the least-happy subgroup.

The important practical point: **money continues to matter, but with diminishing marginal returns.** A salary bump that materially changes housing, family security, retirement optionality, or freedom can matter a lot. A modest bump on top of already-high compensation may matter less than losing work you find deeply absorbing.

## 4. Job satisfaction is meaningfully related to overall life satisfaction

This is not just “work happiness” in a narrow sense.

Meta-analyses such as **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and later work by **Bowling et al.** find a moderate relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction. The causal direction is partly bidirectional: happier people experience jobs more positively, but better jobs also seem to improve well-being.

Given that work occupies a huge fraction of waking life, daily work quality has real spillover into general happiness.

## 5. Autonomy, competence, and relatedness are especially important

Self-determination theory is relevant here. It says well-being is supported by satisfaction of three basic psychological needs:

- autonomy  
- competence/mastery  
- relatedness  

Workplace meta-analyses, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, find that satisfaction of these needs at work predicts engagement, job satisfaction, and well-being; frustration of these needs predicts burnout and ill-being.

This is useful for your case because both options may satisfy different needs:

- Current OpenAI role: likely competence/mastery, technical interest, meaning, perhaps autonomy.  
- Google team-lead role: potentially autonomy, relatedness, mentoring, impact, status, skill growth.  

So the question is not simply “interesting vs. less interesting.” It is: **which role better satisfies autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaning, and sustainable challenge?**

## 6. Leadership can be rewarding, but also increases demands

Leading a small team may be a real positive if it gives you:

- more control over direction  
- mentoring opportunities  
- broader impact  
- a path toward the kind of career identity you want  
- new skills and growth  

But management also often brings:

- more meetings  
- stakeholder management  
- less deep technical time  
- hiring/performance issues  
- ambiguity and emotional labor  

The job-demands/resources literature, e.g. **Bakker & Demerouti’s Job Demands–Resources model**, suggests that high demands are much less harmful when paired with high resources: autonomy, support, feedback, control, and meaning. Challenge demands can be motivating; hindrance demands tend to burn people out.

So “leading a team” is not automatically better or worse. The daily calendar matters enormously.

## 7. Regret research: people often regret missed career/education opportunities

There is less direct research on “regretting staying in the more interesting job,” but regret research gives some clues.

- **Roese & Summerville 2005**, “What We Regret Most…”: education and career are among the most common domains of life regret.  
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.

Implication: if leading a team is something you have “always wanted to do,” there is some regret risk in never testing that path. But this does not imply you should take any management offer. It suggests you should take seriously whether the opportunity is a self-concordant goal, not merely a title/pay/status upgrade.

A useful distinction:

- “I want to lead because I want to develop people, set direction, own outcomes” → likely intrinsic/self-concordant.  
- “I want to lead because it is the next prestige step” → more extrinsic; weaker link to well-being.

## 8. Evidence-based way to frame your decision

Based on the research, I would compare the offers on these dimensions rather than only salary/title:

| Dimension | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Daily intellectual interest | Strong predictor of engagement/satisfaction |
| Autonomy over roadmap | One of the strongest job-design predictors |
| Skill growth | Supports competence/mastery |
| Task significance/mission | Predicts meaning and motivation |
| Quality of manager/coworkers | Social support is highly predictive |
| Actual calendar | People often underweight daily lived experience |
| Stress/hours/politics | High hindrance demands predict burnout |
| Financial impact | Matters more if it changes real life constraints |
| Reversibility/options | Important for regret minimization |
| Leadership fit | Could be intrinsically rewarding if self-concordant |

## Bottom-line reading of the literature

If the Google role is **only** “more money + title, but substantially less intrinsically interesting,” and you are already financially comfortable, the research would generally predict a meaningful risk of lower job satisfaction.

If the Google role is “less technically/research interesting, but much higher autonomy, people development, ownership, and long-term career fit,” then it may still score highly on the variables that predict well-being.

So the empirical answer is: **interesting work matters a lot, often more than moderate additional pay, but leadership itself can be an important form of interesting work if it satisfies autonomy, mastery, relatedness, and meaning.**

answer:
I don’t know of a clean study that exactly answers: “people choosing a more interesting job vs. a higher-paying/managerial but less interesting job, followed for years, measuring regret.” That precise counterfactual is hard. But several adjacent literatures are relevant and fairly consistent.

## Short version

Research generally suggests:

1. **The content of the work itself — interest, autonomy, challenge, skill use, meaning — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but its marginal effect on happiness/job satisfaction is usually smaller, especially once someone is already financially comfortable.**
3. **Leadership/management can be highly satisfying if it increases autonomy, impact, learning, and identity fit — but not if it mostly replaces intrinsically motivating work with coordination, politics, and stress.**
4. **Direct evidence on regret is limited.** General regret research suggests people often regret foregone opportunities, but that does not clearly imply “take the leadership offer” or “stay with the interesting work.” It depends on what opportunity would feel more identity-relevant to you long-term.

So if the Google role is only “a bit more salary” but substantially less interesting, the literature would usually predict lower day-to-day job satisfaction. If “leading a small team” is itself a deeply motivating next step for you, then the role may not be less intrinsically interesting overall — it may just be interesting in a different way.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is a major driver of job satisfaction

The closest research constructs are **intrinsic motivation**, **job characteristics**, **person–job fit**, **meaningful work**, and **autonomy/competence/relatedness**.

### Job Characteristics Model

Classic work by **Hackman & Oldham** proposed that jobs are more motivating when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses such as **Fried & Ferris, 1987** and **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** generally support this. These job characteristics are consistently associated with:

- higher job satisfaction  
- higher internal motivation  
- lower turnover intentions  
- better performance, though performance effects are usually smaller than satisfaction effects  

In many studies, **autonomy** and **skill variety/problem-solving** are among the stronger predictors. “Interesting work” often maps onto exactly these: novelty, challenge, problem-solving, freedom, and using valued skills.

### Person–job fit

A large meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction and moderately related to commitment, performance, and lower intent to quit. In plain English: people are much happier when their job uses the abilities and interests they actually care about.

For your case, the key question is not “research vs. Google” but:

> Which role better fits the kind of problems, skills, pace, autonomy, and identity you want to inhabit most days?

---

## 2. Pay matters, but less than people often expect at high income levels

There is a robust finding that **income is related to well-being**, but usually with **diminishing marginal returns**.

### Pay and job satisfaction

A useful meta-analysis is **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, on pay and job satisfaction. They found that pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the relationship is fairly modest. The correlation between pay level and overall job satisfaction was around **r ≈ .15**, stronger for pay satisfaction specifically but still not huge.

That does **not** mean pay is irrelevant. It matters more when it affects:

- financial stress  
- housing/location  
- family obligations  
- perceived fairness  
- freedom/optionality  
- retirement timeline  
- ability to take future risks  

But once both options are already in the “very comfortable” range, a moderate pay increase may have less impact on daily happiness than work content, autonomy, coworkers, manager quality, and hours.

### Income and subjective well-being

On broader happiness rather than job satisfaction:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that life evaluation rises with income, while daily emotional well-being plateaued around a high-but-not-extreme income level in their dataset.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically, without a sharp plateau.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being continues rising with income, but for the least happy group it may flatten after a point.

The takeaway is not “money stops mattering.” It is:

> Additional money keeps helping, but the effect is usually much smaller than people intuitively expect once basic comfort and security are already met.

For a high-paid tech/research role, “a bit higher salary” is likely to be a weaker happiness predictor than a major change in daily work quality.

---

## 3. Autonomy, mastery, and meaning are especially important

Self-Determination Theory — associated with **Deci & Ryan** — argues that people thrive when work satisfies three psychological needs:

- **autonomy**: I have real agency.  
- **competence**: I am growing and doing difficult things well.  
- **relatedness**: I am connected to people I respect/care about.  

Reviews/meta-analyses such as **Van den Broeck et al., 2016** and **Deci, Olafsen & Ryan, 2017** find that satisfaction of these needs at work predicts better well-being, engagement, performance, and lower burnout.

This is highly relevant because your choice is not just:

> interesting work vs. salary

It may be:

> intellectual interest/autonomy/mastery as an IC researcher  
> vs.  
> leadership/autonomy/impact/mentorship/status as a manager/lead

If leading a team is a genuine source of autonomy, competence, and meaning for you, it could offset a decline in technical/research interest. But if “leading a team” means less agency, more meetings, more politics, and less contact with the problems you care about, the research would predict a risk of lower satisfaction.

---

## 4. Meaningful work has strong associations with well-being

Research on **meaningful work** also points in the same direction. Meta-analytic work, e.g. **Allan et al., 2019**, finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- job satisfaction  
- engagement  
- organizational commitment  
- life satisfaction  
- lower withdrawal intentions  

Again, causality is tricky — happier people may perceive more meaning — but the consistency is notable.

In your case, “interesting” may mean several things:

- intellectually beautiful problems  
- mission importance  
- rare research environment  
- colleagues who stretch you  
- being near the frontier  
- personal identity as a researcher  
- future career capital  

Those are not luxuries. In the work-psychology literature, they are central ingredients of high-quality work.

---

## 5. People often adapt to title/salary faster than to daily work conditions

There is research on **affective forecasting** showing that people often mispredict what will make them happy. See work by **Gilbert, Wilson**, and others. One common error is the **focusing illusion**: when considering a change, we overweight the most salient difference.

In job decisions, salient differences are often:

- salary  
- company prestige  
- title  
- “I’ll lead a team”  
- “I’ll work at X”  

But daily happiness is often driven by less flashy things:

- What will I do on a normal Tuesday?  
- Who will I talk to?  
- How much autonomy do I have?  
- Am I learning?  
- Do I respect the work?  
- Am I exhausted or energized?  
- Is my manager good?  
- Do I have time to think?  

The **Day Reconstruction Method** work by **Kahneman et al.** also supports the idea that moment-to-moment well-being is heavily shaped by actual activities and social interactions, not just global life circumstances.

So the research-informed move is to compare the two roles at the level of a real week, not the offer-sheet level.

---

## 6. Job changes often have a “honeymoon–hangover” pattern

Studies on job change, e.g. **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005**, find a “honeymoon–hangover” effect: people often experience a boost in satisfaction after changing jobs, but some of that boost fades as novelty wears off and reality sets in.

This matters because a new offer can feel good partly because it represents:

- validation  
- optionality  
- status  
- escape from current frustrations  
- imagined future self  

Those effects are real, but they may decay. What remains is the actual work design.

So if you evaluate the Google role, it is worth trying to strip away the novelty premium and ask:

> Once the excitement of being recruited and becoming a lead wears off, would I prefer that actual job?

---

## 7. Evidence on regret is indirect

On regret, there is less direct occupational evidence. General regret research by **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that major life regrets often cluster around domains like education, career, romance, and family. A broad pattern in regret research is that people often regret **inaction** and missed opportunities, especially over long horizons.

But that does not cleanly imply “take the Google job.” It could mean either:

- You might regret not trying leadership when you had the chance.
- Or you might regret leaving unusually interesting frontier research for a less intrinsically compelling role.

The more useful implication is:

> Regret tends to be strongest when a decision conflicts with one’s identity or leaves a valued path unexplored.

So ask which future regret seems more plausible:

1. “I stayed too long as an IC doing fascinating work but never learned to lead.”  
2. “I took a higher-status/better-paid lead role and gave up the most interesting work I’d ever had.”  

Research does not answer that for you, but it suggests the relevant axis is **identity and opportunity**, not just compensation.

---

## 8. A compact research-informed decision frame

Based on the literature, I would compare the roles on these dimensions:

| Factor | Research implication |
|---|---|
| Intrinsic interest of daily work | Very important for job satisfaction |
| Autonomy/control | One of the strongest predictors of motivation and well-being |
| Skill growth/mastery | Strong predictor of engagement |
| Meaning/mission | Strong association with satisfaction and commitment |
| Quality of colleagues/manager | Very important, sometimes underestimated |
| Leadership/impact | Can be deeply motivating if it increases autonomy/meaning |
| Workload/stress/meetings | Can erode benefits of pay/title |
| Salary | Matters, but marginal gains shrink at high income |
| Future optionality | Important, but harder to measure |
| Identity fit | Strongly related to long-term satisfaction/regret |

A useful empirical-style question is:

> If the salaries were identical, which job would I choose?

Then separately:

> How much real life improvement does the salary difference buy me?

If the salary difference meaningfully changes your financial security or life plans, it deserves weight. If it is mostly “nice to have,” the research would usually put more weight on work content, autonomy, learning, and meaning.

---

## Bottom line

The research does **not** say “always choose the more interesting job.” But it does say that **interesting, autonomous, meaningful, skill-stretching work is a central determinant of job satisfaction**, often more predictive than pay once financial comfort is reached.

For your specific tradeoff, the key is whether the Google role is truly “less interesting,” or whether it offers a different kind of intrinsic motivation through leadership, mentorship, ownership, and impact. If it is mostly more money/title with substantially less compelling daily work, the evidence would make me cautious. If it is a credible path into a form of leadership you deeply want, the research would treat that as a serious intrinsic factor, not merely an extrinsic perk.

Then there’s the idea of ideal-self regrets, suggesting those can be most enduring. I’m curious about whether people who stay in fulfilling jobs end up happier or regret their choices since there doesn't seem to be a direct study on that.

On work design, autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support show moderate to large correlations with satisfaction, ranging from .3 to .5. Intrinsic aspirations indicate that autonomous motivation relates to well-being. answer:
There isn’t a clean study of “take more interesting IC research job vs. slightly higher-paid team-lead job,” but several research literatures are relevant. The short version is:

**Interesting/autonomous/meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction, and job satisfaction is substantially related to overall life satisfaction. Pay matters, but once you are already well-paid, modest pay differences usually predict much less happiness than day-to-day work quality, autonomy, fit, manager/team quality, and stress. However, leading a team is not merely an extrinsic perk—it may itself satisfy autonomy, growth, impact, and identity needs.**

## 1. “The work itself” is a major driver of job satisfaction

Classic work-design research finds that jobs are more satisfying when they provide:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task significance / meaning**
- **Task identity**
- **Feedback**
- **Opportunities to learn and use competence**

This comes from the Job Characteristics Model, Hackman & Oldham, and later meta-analyses. For example, **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** meta-analyzed work-design studies and found that autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support were all meaningfully associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower strain. Many of these correlations are in the moderate range, often roughly **r ≈ .3–.5** depending on the feature and measure.

In plain English: people tend to like jobs more when the daily work is engaging, self-directed, challenging in a good way, and connected to something that matters.

For a research engineer, “interesting work” likely bundles several of these: novelty, skill use, autonomy, intellectual challenge, visible progress, and identity fit.

## 2. Job satisfaction is not “just work”; it spills into life satisfaction

Meta-analyses generally find a sizeable association between job satisfaction and overall life satisfaction. Older and newer estimates vary, but a common range is roughly **r ≈ .3–.4+**. See **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** and **Bowling et al. 2010**.

Causality goes both ways: happier people perceive jobs more positively, but job quality also appears to affect life satisfaction. So staying in more satisfying work is not guaranteed to make you happier, but the evidence does not support treating work satisfaction as a minor factor. It is a large part of adult waking life.

## 3. Pay matters, but absolute pay is usually a weaker predictor than people expect

A useful result: **Judge et al. 2010**, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction, found that **pay level was only weakly related to job satisfaction**, around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than raw pay, but even there the relationship is not enormous.

For general well-being, income has a positive relationship with life evaluation and, according to newer work such as **Killingsworth 2021** and the **Kahneman/Killingsworth/Mellers 2023 adversarial collaboration**, experienced well-being often continues rising with income, but roughly with **log income**—meaning each additional dollar matters less at higher income levels.

So if the Google offer is only “a bit higher salary” and you are already financially comfortable, the expected happiness gain from salary alone is probably modest. Exceptions: if the extra money materially changes your life—housing, family obligations, debt, visa/security concerns, ability to take future risks—then it may matter more.

## 4. Intrinsic motivation and person-job fit are robustly important

Research on **Self-Determination Theory**—Deci & Ryan; workplace meta-analyses such as **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**—finds that people do better and feel better when work satisfies:

- **Autonomy**: I choose how to act.
- **Competence**: I am growing and effective.
- **Relatedness**: I have good human connection.
- Sometimes also **meaning/purpose**, though that is often treated separately.

Similarly, person-environment fit research finds that people are more satisfied when their job matches their interests, values, and abilities. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** meta-analyzed person-environment fit and found strong relationships between fit and job satisfaction, commitment, and lower turnover intentions. Person-job fit is often one of the stronger predictors of satisfaction.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- If “frontier research / technically interesting work” is central to your identity and motivation, leaving it may be costly.
- If “leading and developing a team” is a long-standing identity goal, the Google role may improve fit even if the technical work is less interesting.

The key is that “interesting” should not mean only “technically fascinating.” Management can be intrinsically interesting if you enjoy strategy, mentoring, coordination, judgment, and building people/systems. It can also be deeply boring if you mostly value technical exploration.

## 5. Knowledge-work studies emphasize daily progress in meaningful work

For creative/knowledge workers, **Amabile & Kramer’s “progress principle”** work is relevant. In diary studies of professionals, one of the strongest predictors of positive “inner work life” was making progress on meaningful work. Positive affect and perceived progress also relate to creativity.

This is especially relevant for research/engineering roles: a job that gives you regular intellectual progress may be more satisfying than a higher-status role where progress is bureaucratic, ambiguous, or disconnected from what you care about.

## 6. Regret research is less directly decisive, but suggests “missed identity goals” matter

There is not, as far as I know, a strong direct literature saying “people regret leaving interesting jobs” or “people regret not taking management opportunities.” But general regret research has useful patterns:

- **Gilovich & Medvec 1994/1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions—things they did not try.
- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: education and career are among the most common life-regret domains.
- **Davidai & Gilovich 2018**: enduring regrets often involve failures to live up to one’s “ideal self” more than failures of duty.

Interpretation for your situation: regret risk is high when a choice blocks a salient “possible self.” If “I always wanted to lead a team” is a real ideal-self goal, not trying management could become a regret. If “I want to spend my scarce career years on the most interesting AI research problems” is the stronger ideal-self goal, leaving may become the regret.

So the regret literature does not imply “take the Google offer”; it implies you should identify which unrealized path would feel more like a true missed calling.

## 7. A research-informed way to compare the offers

Rather than compare “OpenAI = interesting” vs. “Google = salary/manager,” compare predicted satisfaction on these dimensions:

| Dimension | Research relevance | Ask yourself |
|---|---|---|
| Autonomy | Strong predictor of satisfaction | Which role gives me more real decision rights? |
| Competence/growth | Core SDT need | Where will I learn more of what I want to learn? |
| Meaning/impact | Strongly tied to engagement | Which work would I be proud of in 3 years? |
| Daily interest | Work-design/job-fit literature | What will a normal Tuesday feel like? |
| Relatedness/team | Important for well-being | Which team/manager environment is healthier? |
| Stress/demands | Burnout literature | Which role has worse chronic strain? |
| Identity fit | Regret/person-job fit | Which path feels more like “me”? |
| Optionality | Regret mitigation | Which choice is more reversible? |

My evidence-weighted read would be:

- **Do not overweight a modest salary increase** if you are already financially secure.
- **Do take the leadership opportunity seriously**, because it may satisfy deep intrinsic motives.
- **Be very cautious about giving up genuinely interesting daily work**, because work content, autonomy, and fit are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.
- The crux is whether the Google role is “less interesting technically but more interesting as leadership/growth/impact” or simply “less interesting plus more meetings/status/pay.”

If you can, get concrete data: expected calendar, technical vs. managerial split, decision authority, team quality, manager quality, promotion expectations, and whether you can return to high-end IC/research work if management is not for you. The research says those daily realities will matter more than the abstract brand/salary/title comparison.
